--- Docker workflow ---
     1	# Docker Container Image Build and Release Workflow
     2	#
     3	# This workflow builds, scans, signs, and publishes container images
     4	# for the EVE Frontier microservices when a version tag is pushed.
     5	#
     6	# Security features:
     7	# - Trivy vulnerability scanning (CRITICAL, HIGH severity)
     8	# - Cosign keyless signing using GitHub OIDC
     9	# - SBOM generation with syft
    10	# - Multi-architecture builds (amd64, arm64)
    11	#
    12	# Triggered on: Push of tags matching v*
    13	#
    14	# Required permissions:
    15	# - contents: read
    16	# - packages: write
    17	# - id-token: write (for cosign OIDC)
    18	# - security-events: write (for Trivy results)
    19	
    20	name: Docker Release
    21	
    22	on:
    23	  push:
    24	    tags:
    25	      - 'v*'
    26	  workflow_dispatch:
    27	    inputs:
    28	      tag:
    29	        description: 'Version tag to build (e.g., v0.1.0)'
    30	        required: true
    31	        type: string
    32	
    33	# Workflow-level permissions: minimal by default (principle of least privilege)
    34	# Each job explicitly declares the permissions it needs
    35	permissions: {}
    36	
    37	env:
    38	  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
    39	  IMAGE_PREFIX: ghcr.io/scetrov/evefrontier-rs
    40	
    41	jobs:
    42	  build-binaries:
    43	    name: Build Binaries
    44	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    45	    permissions:
    46	      contents: read
    47	
    48	    strategy:
    49	      matrix:
    50	        target:
    51	          - x86_64-unknown-linux-musl
    52	          - aarch64-unknown-linux-musl
    53	        include:
    54	          - target: x86_64-unknown-linux-musl
    55	            arch: amd64
    56	          - target: aarch64-unknown-linux-musl
    57	            arch: arm64
    58	
    59	    outputs:
    60	      version: ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}
    61	
    62	    steps:
    63	      - name: Checkout code
    64	        uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0 # v7.0.0
    65	
    66	      - name: Determine version
    67	        id: version
    68	        env:
    69	          INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
    70	        run: |
    71	          if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" = "workflow_dispatch" ]; then
    72	            if [[ ! "$INPUT_TAG" =~ ^v[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+(-[0-9A-Za-z][0-9A-Za-z.-]*)?(\+[0-9A-Za-z][0-9A-Za-z.-]*)?$ ]]; then
    73	              echo "workflow_dispatch tag must be a v-prefixed semantic version" >&2
    74	              exit 1
    75	            fi
    76	            VERSION="$INPUT_TAG"
    77	          else
    78	            VERSION="${{ github.ref_name }}"
    79	          fi
    80	          # Strip 'v' prefix to match docker/metadata-action semver output
    81	          VERSION="${VERSION#v}"
    82	          echo "version=${VERSION}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
    83	          echo "Building version: ${VERSION}"
    84	
    85	      - name: Install Rust toolchain
    86	        uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@a54c7afa936fefeb4456b2dd8068152669aa8203 # stable
    87	        with:
    88	          toolchain: "1.97.0"
    89	          targets: ${{ matrix.target }}
    90	
    91	      - name: Install cargo-zigbuild
    92	        run: |
    93	          cargo install cargo-zigbuild --locked
    94	
    95	      - name: Install zig
    96	        uses: goto-bus-stop/setup-zig@abea47f85e598557f500fa1fd2ab7464fcb39406 # v2.2.1
    97	        with:
    98	          version: 0.13.0
    99	
   100	      - name: Build services
   101	        run: |
   102	          cargo zigbuild --release --target ${{ matrix.target }} \
   103	            -p evefrontier-service-route \
   104	            -p evefrontier-service-scout-gates \
   105	            -p evefrontier-service-scout-range
   106	
   107	      - name: Create artifact directory
   108	        run: |
   109	          mkdir -p artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}
   110	          cp target/${{ matrix.target }}/release/evefrontier-service-route artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}/
   111	          cp target/${{ matrix.target }}/release/evefrontier-service-scout-gates artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}/
   112	          cp target/${{ matrix.target }}/release/evefrontier-service-scout-range artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}/
   113	
   114	      - name: Upload artifacts
   115	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@043fb46d1a93c77aae656e7c1c64a875d1fc6a0a # v7.0.1
   116	        with:
   117	          name: binaries-${{ matrix.arch }}
   118	          path: artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}
   119	          retention-days: 1
   120	
   121	  build-images:
   122	    name: Build and Push Images
   123	    needs: build-binaries
   124	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   125	    permissions:
   126	      contents: read
   127	      packages: write
   128	      id-token: write
   129	      security-events: write
   130	
   131	    strategy:
   132	      matrix:
   133	        service:
   134	          - route
   135	          - scout-gates
   136	          - scout-range
   137	
   138	    steps:
   139	      - name: Checkout code
   140	        uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0 # v7.0.0
   141	
   142	      - name: Download amd64 binaries
   143	        uses: actions/download-artifact@3e5f45b2cfb9172054b4087a40e8e0b5a5461e7c # v8.0.1
   144	        with:
   145	          name: binaries-amd64
   146	          path: artifacts/amd64
   147	
   148	      - name: Download arm64 binaries
   149	        uses: actions/download-artifact@3e5f45b2cfb9172054b4087a40e8e0b5a5461e7c # v8.0.1
   150	        with:
   151	          name: binaries-arm64
   152	          path: artifacts/arm64
   153	
   154	      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
   155	        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@bb05f3f5519dd87d3ba754cc423b652a5edd6d2c # v4.2.0
   156	
   157	      - name: Login to GitHub Container Registry
   158	        uses: docker/login-action@af1e73f918a031802d376d3c8bbc3fe56130a9b0 # v4.4.0
   159	        with:
   160	          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
   161	          username: ${{ github.actor }}
   162	          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
   163	
   164	      - name: Extract metadata
   165	        id: meta
   166	        uses: docker/metadata-action@dc802804100637a589fabce1cb79ff13a1411302 # v6.2.0
   167	        with:
   168	          images: ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-${{ matrix.service }}
   169	          tags: |
   170	            type=semver,pattern={{version}}
   171	            type=semver,pattern={{major}}.{{minor}}
   172	            type=semver,pattern={{major}}
   173	            type=raw,value=latest,enable=${{ !contains(github.ref, '-') }}
   174	
   175	      - name: Create multi-arch Dockerfile
   176	        run: |
   177	          cat > Dockerfile.multiarch <<'EOF'
   178	          FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12:nonroot
   179	          ARG TARGETARCH
   180	          ARG SERVICE
   181	          COPY artifacts/${TARGETARCH}/evefrontier-service-${SERVICE} /service
   182	          USER 65532:65532
   183	          EXPOSE 8080
   184	          ENTRYPOINT ["/service"]
   185	          EOF
   186	
   187	      - name: Build and push multi-arch image
   188	        id: build
   189	        uses: docker/build-push-action@53b7df96c91f9c12dcc8a07bcb9ccacbed38856a # v7.3.0
   190	        with:
   191	          context: .
   192	          file: Dockerfile.multiarch
   193	          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
   194	          push: true
   195	          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
   196	          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
   197	          provenance: mode=max
   198	          build-args: |
   199	            SERVICE=${{ matrix.service }}
   200	
   201	      - name: Log image digest
   202	        run: echo "Image digest for ${{ matrix.service }}:${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }} is ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}"
   203	
   204	  scan-images:
   205	    name: Scan Images
   206	    needs: [build-binaries, build-images]
   207	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   208	    permissions:
   209	      contents: read
   210	      security-events: write
   211	
   212	    strategy:
   213	      matrix:
   214	        service:
   215	          - route
   216	          - scout-gates
   217	          - scout-range
   218	
   219	    steps:
   220	      - name: Run Trivy vulnerability scanner
   221	        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@ed142fd0673e97e23eac54620cfb913e5ce36c25 # v0.36.0
   222	        with:
   223	          image-ref: '${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-${{ matrix.service }}:${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }}'
   224	          format: 'sarif'
   225	          output: 'trivy-results-${{ matrix.service }}.sarif'
   226	          severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH'
   227	          exit-code: '1'
   228	          # NOTE: We ignore "unfixed" vulnerabilities in the container base image.
   229	          # - Rust dependencies are scanned separately with `cargo-audit`.
   230	          # - Trivy scans the full container image (including the distroless base).
   231	          # - "unfixed" here means no upstream vendor patch exists yet; we rely on
   232	          #   the Google-maintained distroless base image to ship security fixes.
   233	          # - We still fail the build on CRITICAL/HIGH issues when a fix is available.
   234	          #
   235	          # For the full security policy, rationale, and the process for monitoring
   236	          # when fixes become available, see:
   237	          #   - docs/SECURITY_AUDIT.md (container image scanning section)
   238	          #   - docs/adrs/0007-devsecops-practices.md (DevSecOps practices ADR)
   239	          ignore-unfixed: true
   240	
   241	      - name: Upload Trivy scan results
   242	        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@7188fc363630916deb702c7fdcf4e481b751f97a # v4
   243	        if: always()
   244	        with:
   245	          sarif_file: 'trivy-results-${{ matrix.service }}.sarif'
   246	          category: 'trivy-${{ matrix.service }}'
   247	
   248	  sign-images:
   249	    name: Sign Images
   250	    needs: [build-binaries, build-images, scan-images]
   251	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   252	    permissions:
   253	      contents: read
   254	      packages: write
   255	      id-token: write
   256	
   257	    strategy:
   258	      matrix:
   259	        service:
   260	          - route
   261	          - scout-gates
   262	          - scout-range
   263	
   264	    steps:
   265	      - name: Install cosign
   266	        uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@6f9f17788090df1f26f669e9d70d6ae9567deba6 # v4.1.2
   267	
   268	      - name: Login to GitHub Container Registry
   269	        uses: docker/login-action@af1e73f918a031802d376d3c8bbc3fe56130a9b0 # v4.4.0
   270	        with:
   271	          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
   272	          username: ${{ github.actor }}
   273	          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
   274	
   275	      - name: Sign container image
   276	        env:
   277	          COSIGN_EXPERIMENTAL: "true"
   278	        run: |
   279	          cosign sign --yes ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-${{ matrix.service }}:${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }}
   280	
   281	  generate-sbom:
   282	    name: Generate SBOM
   283	    needs: [build-binaries, build-images]
   284	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   285	    permissions:
   286	      contents: read
   287	      packages: read
   288	
   289	    strategy:
   290	      matrix:
   291	        service:
   292	          - route
   293	          - scout-gates
   294	          - scout-range
   295	
   296	    steps:
   297	      - name: Install syft
   298	        uses: anchore/sbom-action/download-syft@e22c389904149dbc22b58101806040fa8d37a610 # v0.24.0
   299	
   300	      - name: Generate SBOM
   301	        run: |
   302	          syft ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-${{ matrix.service }}:${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }} \
   303	            -o spdx-json=sbom-${{ matrix.service }}.spdx.json \
   304	            -o cyclonedx-json=sbom-${{ matrix.service }}.cyclonedx.json
   305	
   306	      - name: Upload SBOM artifacts
   307	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@043fb46d1a93c77aae656e7c1c64a875d1fc6a0a # v7.0.1
   308	        with:
   309	          name: sbom-${{ matrix.service }}
   310	          path: |
   311	            sbom-${{ matrix.service }}.spdx.json
   312	            sbom-${{ matrix.service }}.cyclonedx.json
   313	          retention-days: 90
   314	
   315	  summary:
   316	    name: Release Summary
   317	    needs: [build-binaries, build-images, scan-images, sign-images, generate-sbom]
   318	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   319	    permissions: {}
   320	    if: always()
   321	
   322	    steps:
   323	      - name: Generate summary
   324	        run: |
   325	          VERSION="${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }}"
   326	
   327	          cat >> "$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY" <<EOF
   328	          # Docker Release Summary
   329	
   330	          **Version**: ${VERSION}
   331	          **Registry**: ghcr.io
   332	
   333	          ## Images
   334	
   335	          | Service | Image | Status |
   336	          |---------|-------|--------|
   337	          | route | \`${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-route:${VERSION}\` | ${{ needs.build-images.result == 'success' && '✅' || '❌' }} |
   338	          | scout-gates | \`${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-scout-gates:${VERSION}\` | ${{ needs.build-images.result == 'success' && '✅' || '❌' }} |
   339	          | scout-range | \`${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-scout-range:${VERSION}\` | ${{ needs.build-images.result == 'success' && '✅' || '❌' }} |
   340	
   341	          ## Security
   342	
   343	          | Check | Status |
   344	          |-------|--------|
   345	          | Trivy Scan | ${{ needs.scan-images.result == 'success' && '✅ Passed' || '❌ Failed' }} |
   346	          | Cosign Signing | ${{ needs.sign-images.result == 'success' && '✅ Signed' || '❌ Failed' }} |
   347	          | SBOM Generation | ${{ needs.generate-sbom.result == 'success' && '✅ Generated' || '❌ Failed' }} |
   348	
   349	          ## Verification
   350	
   351	          To verify image signatures:
   352	
   353	          \`\`\`bash
   354	          # Install cosign
   355	          # https://docs.sigstore.dev/cosign/installation/
   356	
   357	          # Verify signature (ensures image was built by this repository's release workflow)
   358	          cosign verify \\
   359	            --certificate-identity="https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}/.github/workflows/docker-release.yml@refs/tags/${VERSION}" \\
   360	            --certificate-oidc-issuer="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" \\
   361	            ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-route:${VERSION}
   362	          \`\`\`
   363	
   364	          ## Pull Commands
   365	
   366	          \`\`\`bash
   367	          docker pull ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-route:${VERSION}
   368	          docker pull ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-scout-gates:${VERSION}
   369	          docker pull ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-scout-range:${VERSION}
   370	          \`\`\`
   371	          EOF

--- crates/evefrontier-service-route/Dockerfile ---
     1	# Build stage
     2	FROM rust:1.97.0-bookworm AS builder
     3	
     4	WORKDIR /app
     5	
     6	# Install cross-compilation tools for smaller binaries
     7	RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
     8	    musl-tools \
     9	    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
    10	
    11	# Copy workspace configuration
    12	COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
    13	
    14	# Copy workspace root src (required for workspace package)
    15	COPY src/ src/
    16	
    17	# Copy all workspace crates
    18	COPY crates/ crates/
    19	
    20	# Build the route service with release optimizations
    21	RUN cargo build --release -p evefrontier-service-route
    22	
    23	# Strip debug symbols for smaller binary
    24	RUN strip /app/target/release/evefrontier-service-route
    25	
    26	# Runtime stage - using Distroless for minimal attack surface
    27	FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12:nonroot
    28	
    29	# Copy the binary from builder
    30	COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/evefrontier-service-route /usr/local/bin/evefrontier-service-route
    31	
    32	# Set non-root user (distroless:nonroot is UID 65532)
    33	USER nonroot
    34	
    35	# Expose the default service port
    36	EXPOSE 8080
    37	
    38	# Set default environment variables
    39	ENV SERVICE_PORT=8080
    40	ENV RUST_LOG=info
    41	
    42	# Run the service
    43	ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/evefrontier-service-route"]
--- crates/evefrontier-service-scout-gates/Dockerfile ---
     1	# Build stage
     2	FROM rust:1.97.0-bookworm AS builder
     3	
     4	WORKDIR /app
     5	
     6	# Install cross-compilation tools for smaller binaries
     7	RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
     8	    musl-tools \
     9	    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
    10	
    11	# Copy workspace configuration
    12	COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
    13	
    14	# Copy workspace root src (required for workspace package)
    15	COPY src/ src/
    16	
    17	# Copy all workspace crates
    18	COPY crates/ crates/
    19	
    20	# Build the scout-gates service with release optimizations
    21	RUN cargo build --release -p evefrontier-service-scout-gates
    22	
    23	# Strip debug symbols for smaller binary
    24	RUN strip /app/target/release/evefrontier-service-scout-gates
    25	
    26	# Runtime stage - using Distroless for minimal attack surface
    27	FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12:nonroot
    28	
    29	# Copy the binary from builder
    30	COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/evefrontier-service-scout-gates /usr/local/bin/evefrontier-service-scout-gates
    31	
    32	# Set non-root user (distroless:nonroot is UID 65532)
    33	USER nonroot
    34	
    35	# Expose the default service port
    36	EXPOSE 8080
    37	
    38	# Set default environment variables
    39	ENV SERVICE_PORT=8080
    40	ENV RUST_LOG=info
    41	
    42	# Run the service
    43	ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/evefrontier-service-scout-gates"]
--- crates/evefrontier-service-scout-range/Dockerfile ---
     1	# Build stage
     2	FROM rust:1.97.0-bookworm AS builder
     3	
     4	WORKDIR /app
     5	
     6	# Install cross-compilation tools for smaller binaries
     7	RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \
     8	    musl-tools \
     9	    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
    10	
    11	# Copy workspace configuration
    12	COPY Cargo.toml Cargo.lock ./
    13	
    14	# Copy workspace root src (required for workspace package)
    15	COPY src/ src/
    16	
    17	# Copy all workspace crates
    18	COPY crates/ crates/
    19	
    20	# Build the scout-range service with release optimizations
    21	RUN cargo build --release -p evefrontier-service-scout-range
    22	
    23	# Strip debug symbols for smaller binary
    24	RUN strip /app/target/release/evefrontier-service-scout-range
    25	
    26	# Runtime stage - using Distroless for minimal attack surface
    27	FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12:nonroot
    28	
    29	# Copy the binary from builder
    30	COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/evefrontier-service-scout-range /usr/local/bin/evefrontier-service-scout-range
    31	
    32	# Set non-root user (distroless:nonroot is UID 65532)
    33	USER nonroot
    34	
    35	# Expose the default service port
    36	EXPOSE 8080
    37	
    38	# Set default environment variables
    39	ENV SERVICE_PORT=8080
    40	ENV RUST_LOG=info
    41	
    42	# Run the service
    43	ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/evefrontier-service-scout-range"]
--- Docker workflow ---
     1	# Docker Container Image Build and Release Workflow
     2	#
     3	# This workflow builds, scans, signs, and publishes container images
     4	# for the EVE Frontier microservices when a version tag is pushed.
     5	#
     6	# Security features:
     7	# - Trivy vulnerability scanning (CRITICAL, HIGH severity)
     8	# - Cosign keyless signing using GitHub OIDC
     9	# - SBOM generation with syft
    10	# - Multi-architecture builds (amd64, arm64)
    11	#
    12	# Triggered on: Push of tags matching v*
    13	#
    14	# Required permissions:
    15	# - contents: read
    16	# - packages: write
    17	# - id-token: write (for cosign OIDC)
    18	# - security-events: write (for Trivy results)
    19	
    20	name: Docker Release
    21	
    22	on:
    23	  push:
    24	    tags:
    25	      - 'v*'
    26	  workflow_dispatch:
    27	    inputs:
    28	      tag:
    29	        description: 'Version tag to build (e.g., v0.1.0)'
    30	        required: true
    31	        type: string
    32	
    33	# Workflow-level permissions: minimal by default (principle of least privilege)
    34	# Each job explicitly declares the permissions it needs
    35	permissions: {}
    36	
    37	env:
    38	  REGISTRY: ghcr.io
    39	  IMAGE_PREFIX: ghcr.io/scetrov/evefrontier-rs
    40	
    41	jobs:
    42	  build-binaries:
    43	    name: Build Binaries
    44	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    45	    permissions:
    46	      contents: read
    47	
    48	    strategy:
    49	      matrix:
    50	        target:
    51	          - x86_64-unknown-linux-musl
    52	          - aarch64-unknown-linux-musl
    53	        include:
    54	          - target: x86_64-unknown-linux-musl
    55	            arch: amd64
    56	          - target: aarch64-unknown-linux-musl
    57	            arch: arm64
    58	
    59	    outputs:
    60	      version: ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}
    61	
    62	    steps:
    63	      - name: Checkout code
    64	        uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0 # v7.0.0
    65	
    66	      - name: Determine version
    67	        id: version
    68	        env:
    69	          INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
    70	        run: |
    71	          if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" = "workflow_dispatch" ]; then
    72	            if [[ ! "$INPUT_TAG" =~ ^v[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+(-[0-9A-Za-z][0-9A-Za-z.-]*)?(\+[0-9A-Za-z][0-9A-Za-z.-]*)?$ ]]; then
    73	              echo "workflow_dispatch tag must be a v-prefixed semantic version" >&2
    74	              exit 1
    75	            fi
    76	            VERSION="$INPUT_TAG"
    77	          else
    78	            VERSION="${{ github.ref_name }}"
    79	          fi
    80	          # Strip 'v' prefix to match docker/metadata-action semver output
    81	          VERSION="${VERSION#v}"
    82	          echo "version=${VERSION}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
    83	          echo "Building version: ${VERSION}"
    84	
    85	      - name: Install Rust toolchain
    86	        uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@a54c7afa936fefeb4456b2dd8068152669aa8203 # stable
    87	        with:
    88	          toolchain: "1.97.0"
    89	          targets: ${{ matrix.target }}
    90	
    91	      - name: Install cargo-zigbuild
    92	        run: |
    93	          cargo install cargo-zigbuild --locked
    94	
    95	      - name: Install zig
    96	        uses: goto-bus-stop/setup-zig@abea47f85e598557f500fa1fd2ab7464fcb39406 # v2.2.1
    97	        with:
    98	          version: 0.13.0
    99	
   100	      - name: Build services
   101	        run: |
   102	          cargo zigbuild --release --target ${{ matrix.target }} \
   103	            -p evefrontier-service-route \
   104	            -p evefrontier-service-scout-gates \
   105	            -p evefrontier-service-scout-range
   106	
   107	      - name: Create artifact directory
   108	        run: |
   109	          mkdir -p artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}
   110	          cp target/${{ matrix.target }}/release/evefrontier-service-route artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}/
   111	          cp target/${{ matrix.target }}/release/evefrontier-service-scout-gates artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}/
   112	          cp target/${{ matrix.target }}/release/evefrontier-service-scout-range artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}/
   113	
   114	      - name: Upload artifacts
   115	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@043fb46d1a93c77aae656e7c1c64a875d1fc6a0a # v7.0.1
   116	        with:
   117	          name: binaries-${{ matrix.arch }}
   118	          path: artifacts/${{ matrix.arch }}
   119	          retention-days: 1
   120	
   121	  build-images:
   122	    name: Build and Push Images
   123	    needs: build-binaries
   124	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   125	    permissions:
   126	      contents: read
   127	      packages: write
   128	      id-token: write
   129	      security-events: write
   130	
   131	    strategy:
   132	      matrix:
   133	        service:
   134	          - route
   135	          - scout-gates
   136	          - scout-range
   137	
   138	    steps:
   139	      - name: Checkout code
   140	        uses: actions/checkout@9c091bb21b7c1c1d1991bb908d89e4e9dddfe3e0 # v7.0.0
   141	
   142	      - name: Download amd64 binaries
   143	        uses: actions/download-artifact@3e5f45b2cfb9172054b4087a40e8e0b5a5461e7c # v8.0.1
   144	        with:
   145	          name: binaries-amd64
   146	          path: artifacts/amd64
   147	
   148	      - name: Download arm64 binaries
   149	        uses: actions/download-artifact@3e5f45b2cfb9172054b4087a40e8e0b5a5461e7c # v8.0.1
   150	        with:
   151	          name: binaries-arm64
   152	          path: artifacts/arm64
   153	
   154	      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
   155	        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@bb05f3f5519dd87d3ba754cc423b652a5edd6d2c # v4.2.0
   156	
   157	      - name: Login to GitHub Container Registry
   158	        uses: docker/login-action@af1e73f918a031802d376d3c8bbc3fe56130a9b0 # v4.4.0
   159	        with:
   160	          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
   161	          username: ${{ github.actor }}
   162	          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
   163	
   164	      - name: Extract metadata
   165	        id: meta
   166	        uses: docker/metadata-action@dc802804100637a589fabce1cb79ff13a1411302 # v6.2.0
   167	        with:
   168	          images: ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-${{ matrix.service }}
   169	          tags: |
   170	            type=semver,pattern={{version}}
   171	            type=semver,pattern={{major}}.{{minor}}
   172	            type=semver,pattern={{major}}
   173	            type=raw,value=latest,enable=${{ !contains(github.ref, '-') }}
   174	
   175	      - name: Create multi-arch Dockerfile
   176	        run: |
   177	          cat > Dockerfile.multiarch <<'EOF'
   178	          FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12:nonroot
   179	          ARG TARGETARCH
   180	          ARG SERVICE
   181	          COPY artifacts/${TARGETARCH}/evefrontier-service-${SERVICE} /service
   182	          USER 65532:65532
   183	          EXPOSE 8080
   184	          ENTRYPOINT ["/service"]
   185	          EOF
   186	
   187	      - name: Build and push multi-arch image
   188	        id: build
   189	        uses: docker/build-push-action@53b7df96c91f9c12dcc8a07bcb9ccacbed38856a # v7.3.0
   190	        with:
   191	          context: .
   192	          file: Dockerfile.multiarch
   193	          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
   194	          push: true
   195	          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
   196	          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
   197	          provenance: mode=max
   198	          build-args: |
   199	            SERVICE=${{ matrix.service }}
   200	
   201	      - name: Log image digest
   202	        run: echo "Image digest for ${{ matrix.service }}:${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }} is ${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}"
   203	
   204	  scan-images:
   205	    name: Scan Images
   206	    needs: [build-binaries, build-images]
   207	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   208	    permissions:
   209	      contents: read
   210	      security-events: write
   211	
   212	    strategy:
   213	      matrix:
   214	        service:
   215	          - route
   216	          - scout-gates
   217	          - scout-range
   218	
   219	    steps:
   220	      - name: Run Trivy vulnerability scanner
   221	        uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@ed142fd0673e97e23eac54620cfb913e5ce36c25 # v0.36.0
   222	        with:
   223	          image-ref: '${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-${{ matrix.service }}:${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }}'
   224	          format: 'sarif'
   225	          output: 'trivy-results-${{ matrix.service }}.sarif'
   226	          severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH'
   227	          exit-code: '1'
   228	          # NOTE: We ignore "unfixed" vulnerabilities in the container base image.
   229	          # - Rust dependencies are scanned separately with `cargo-audit`.
   230	          # - Trivy scans the full container image (including the distroless base).
   231	          # - "unfixed" here means no upstream vendor patch exists yet; we rely on
   232	          #   the Google-maintained distroless base image to ship security fixes.
   233	          # - We still fail the build on CRITICAL/HIGH issues when a fix is available.
   234	          #
   235	          # For the full security policy, rationale, and the process for monitoring
   236	          # when fixes become available, see:
   237	          #   - docs/SECURITY_AUDIT.md (container image scanning section)
   238	          #   - docs/adrs/0007-devsecops-practices.md (DevSecOps practices ADR)
   239	          ignore-unfixed: true
   240	
   241	      - name: Upload Trivy scan results
   242	        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@7188fc363630916deb702c7fdcf4e481b751f97a # v4
   243	        if: always()
   244	        with:
   245	          sarif_file: 'trivy-results-${{ matrix.service }}.sarif'
   246	          category: 'trivy-${{ matrix.service }}'
   247	
   248	  sign-images:
   249	    name: Sign Images
   250	    needs: [build-binaries, build-images, scan-images]
   251	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   252	    permissions:
   253	      contents: read
   254	      packages: write
   255	      id-token: write
   256	
   257	    strategy:
   258	      matrix:
   259	        service:
   260	          - route
   261	          - scout-gates
   262	          - scout-range
   263	
   264	    steps:
   265	      - name: Install cosign
   266	        uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@6f9f17788090df1f26f669e9d70d6ae9567deba6 # v4.1.2
   267	
   268	      - name: Login to GitHub Container Registry
   269	        uses: docker/login-action@af1e73f918a031802d376d3c8bbc3fe56130a9b0 # v4.4.0
   270	        with:
   271	          registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}
   272	          username: ${{ github.actor }}
   273	          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
   274	
   275	      - name: Sign container image
   276	        env:
   277	          COSIGN_EXPERIMENTAL: "true"
   278	        run: |
   279	          cosign sign --yes ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-${{ matrix.service }}:${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }}
   280	
   281	  generate-sbom:
   282	    name: Generate SBOM
   283	    needs: [build-binaries, build-images]
   284	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   285	    permissions:
   286	      contents: read
   287	      packages: read
   288	
   289	    strategy:
   290	      matrix:
   291	        service:
   292	          - route
   293	          - scout-gates
   294	          - scout-range
   295	
   296	    steps:
   297	      - name: Install syft
   298	        uses: anchore/sbom-action/download-syft@e22c389904149dbc22b58101806040fa8d37a610 # v0.24.0
   299	
   300	      - name: Generate SBOM
   301	        run: |
   302	          syft ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-${{ matrix.service }}:${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }} \
   303	            -o spdx-json=sbom-${{ matrix.service }}.spdx.json \
   304	            -o cyclonedx-json=sbom-${{ matrix.service }}.cyclonedx.json
   305	
   306	      - name: Upload SBOM artifacts
   307	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@043fb46d1a93c77aae656e7c1c64a875d1fc6a0a # v7.0.1
   308	        with:
   309	          name: sbom-${{ matrix.service }}
   310	          path: |
   311	            sbom-${{ matrix.service }}.spdx.json
   312	            sbom-${{ matrix.service }}.cyclonedx.json
   313	          retention-days: 90
   314	
   315	  summary:
   316	    name: Release Summary
   317	    needs: [build-binaries, build-images, scan-images, sign-images, generate-sbom]
   318	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   319	    permissions: {}
   320	    if: always()
   321	
   322	    steps:
   323	      - name: Generate summary
   324	        run: |
   325	          VERSION="${{ needs.build-binaries.outputs.version }}"
   326	
   327	          cat >> "$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY" <<EOF
   328	          # Docker Release Summary
   329	
   330	          **Version**: ${VERSION}
   331	          **Registry**: ghcr.io
   332	
   333	          ## Images
   334	
   335	          | Service | Image | Status |
   336	          |---------|-------|--------|
   337	          | route | \`${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-route:${VERSION}\` | ${{ needs.build-images.result == 'success' && '✅' || '❌' }} |
   338	          | scout-gates | \`${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-scout-gates:${VERSION}\` | ${{ needs.build-images.result == 'success' && '✅' || '❌' }} |
   339	          | scout-range | \`${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-scout-range:${VERSION}\` | ${{ needs.build-images.result == 'success' && '✅' || '❌' }} |
   340	
   341	          ## Security
   342	
   343	          | Check | Status |
   344	          |-------|--------|
   345	          | Trivy Scan | ${{ needs.scan-images.result == 'success' && '✅ Passed' || '❌ Failed' }} |
   346	          | Cosign Signing | ${{ needs.sign-images.result == 'success' && '✅ Signed' || '❌ Failed' }} |
   347	          | SBOM Generation | ${{ needs.generate-sbom.result == 'success' && '✅ Generated' || '❌ Failed' }} |
   348	
   349	          ## Verification
   350	
   351	          To verify image signatures:
   352	
   353	          \`\`\`bash
   354	          # Install cosign
   355	          # https://docs.sigstore.dev/cosign/installation/
   356	
   357	          # Verify signature (ensures image was built by this repository's release workflow)
   358	          cosign verify \\
   359	            --certificate-identity="https://github.com/${{ github.repository }}/.github/workflows/docker-release.yml@refs/tags/${VERSION}" \\
   360	            --certificate-oidc-issuer="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" \\
   361	            ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-route:${VERSION}
   362	          \`\`\`
   363	
   364	          ## Pull Commands
   365	
   366	          \`\`\`bash
   367	          docker pull ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-route:${VERSION}
   368	          docker pull ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-scout-gates:${VERSION}
   369	          docker pull ${{ env.IMAGE_PREFIX }}/evefrontier-service-scout-range:${VERSION}
   370	          \`\`\`
   371	          EOF

arguments.command:
printf '%s
' '--- current code-scanning Scorecard alerts ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/code-scanning/alerts?state=open&per_page=100' --paginate 2>&1 | jq -r '.[] | select(.tool.name == "OpenSSF Scorecard") | "#\(.number) | \(.rule.id) | severity=\(.rule.security_severity_level // "none") | state=\(.state) | created=\(.created_at) | dismissed=\(.dismissed_at // "") | \(.html_url)"'; printf '%s
' '--- all selected alerts ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/code-scanning/alerts?per_page=100' --paginate 2>&1 | jq -r '.[] | select(.number >= 42 and .number <= 51) | "#\(.number) | \(.tool.name) | \(.rule.id) | state=\(.state) | created=\(.created_at) | updated=\(.updated_at) | dismiss=\(.dismissed_reason // "") | \(.html_url)"'; printf '%s
' '--- branch protection ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/branches/main/protection' 2>&1; printf '%s
' '--- repository security settings ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs' --jq '{archived,disabled,has_discussions,has_issues,has_projects,allow_forking,allow_squash_merge,allow_merge_commit,allow_rebase_merge,delete_branch_on_merge,web_commit_signoff_required,security_and_analysis}' 2>&1

printf '%s
' '--- codeowners ---'; find . -path './.git' -prune -o -iname 'CODEOWNERS' -print -exec nl -ba {} \;; printf '%s
' '--- governance/support/release docs ---'; for f in SECURITY.md CONTRIBUTING.md CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md README.md docs/RELEASE.md docs/TESTING.md docs/DEPLOYMENT.md; do echo "--- $f ---"; grep -nE -i 'review|pull request|maintain|fuzz|security|branch|contribut|disclos|release|support|best practice|cii' "$f" | head -80 || true; done; printf '%s
' '--- fuzz references (excluding node/build/git) ---'; grep -RInE --exclude-dir=.git --exclude-dir=node_modules --exclude-dir=target --exclude='Cargo.lock' 'cargo[- ]fuzz|libfuzzer|fuzzing|fuzzer|honggfuzz|afl' . || true

printf '%s\n' '--- scorecard history/workflow runs ---'; gh run list --repo Scetrov/evefrontier-rs --workflow scorecard.yml --limit 20 --json databaseId,status,conclusion,createdAt,updatedAt,event,headBranch,headSha,url,displayTitle 2>&1; printf '%s\n' '--- public scorecard API ---'; curl -fsSL --max-time 30 'https://api.securityscorecards.dev/projects/github.com/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs' | jq '{date,score,checks: [.checks[] | select(.name == "Pinned-Dependencies" or .name == "Branch-Protection" or .name == "Code-Review" or .name == "CII-Best-Practices" or .name == "Fuzzing") | {name,score,reason,details,documentation}]}' 2>&1

printf '%s\n' '--- recent merged PR review facts ---'; gh pr list --repo Scetrov/evefrontier-rs --state merged --limit 50 --json number,title,author,mergedAt,createdAt,isDraft,url,reviewDecision 2>&1 | jq -r '.[] | "#\(.number) | \(.createdAt) -> \(.mergedAt) | author=\(.author.login) | review=\(.reviewDecision // "none") | \(.title)"'; printf '%s\n' '--- default branch commit / contributors ---'; gh api repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/commits/main --jq '{sha:.sha,commit:.commit.message,author:.author.login,date:.commit.author.date}' 2>&1; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/contributors?per_page=100' --paginate --jq '.[] | {login,contributions}' 2>&1

text: Use context-mode MCP tools (execute, fetch_and_index) instead of inline HTTP clients. Raw curl/wget output floods the context window. For an MCP-down escape hatch, use silent + file output: `curl -s -o /tmp/x.json URL` or `wget -q -O /tmp/x.json URL`.

printf '%s
' '--- repository rulesets ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/rulesets?includes_parents=true' 2>&1; printf '%s
' '--- branch details ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/branches/main' --jq '{name,protected,protection_url,commit:{sha:.commit.sha}}' 2>&1; printf '%s
' '--- actions / vulnerability / secret feature settings ---'; gh api repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs --jq '{has_downloads,has_wiki,has_pages,has_discussions,visibility,security_and_analysis}' 2>&1; printf '%s
' '--- code scanning defaults ---'; gh api repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/code-scanning/default-setup 2>&1

--- #42 ---
{"created_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:22Z","html_url":"https://github.com/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/security/code-scanning/42","most_recent_instance":{"analysis_key":".github/workflows/scorecard.yml:analysis","category":"supply-chain/branch-protection","location":{"end_column":0,"end_line":1,"path":"no file associated with this alert","start_column":1,"start_line":1},"message":"score is 3: branch protection is not maximal on development and all release branches:
Warn: branch 'main' does not require approvers
Warn: codeowners review is required - but no codeowners file found in repo
Warn: 'last push approval' is disabled on branch 'main'
Warn: 'up-to-date branches' is disabled on branch 'main'
Click Remediation section below to solve this issue","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":42,"rule":{"description":"Branch-Protection","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- Enable branch protection settings in your source hosting provider to avoid force pushes or deletion of your important branches.

- For GitHub, check out the steps for rulesets [here](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/creating-rulesets-for-a-repository) and rules [here](https://docs.github.com/en/github/administering-a-repository/managing-a-branch-protection-rule).



**Severity**: High



**Details**:

Risk: `High` (vulnerable to intentional malicious code injection)



This check determines whether a project's default and release branches are

protected with GitHub's [branch protection](https://docs.github.com/github/administering-a-repository/defining-the-mergeability-of-pull-requests/about-protected-branches) 

or [repository rules](https://docs.github.com/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/about-rulesets) settings.

Branch protection allows maintainers to define rules that enforce

certain workflows for branches, such as requiring review or passing certain

status checks before acceptance into a main branch, or preventing rewriting of

public history.



Note: The following settings queried by the Branch-Protection check require an admin token: `DismissStaleReviews`, `EnforceAdmins`, `RequireLastPushApproval`, `RequiresStatusChecks` and `UpToDateBeforeMerge`. If

the provided token does not have admin access, the check will query the branch

settings accessible to non-admins and provide results based only on these settings.

However, all of these settings are accessible via Repo Rules. `EnforceAdmins` is calculated slightly differently.

This setting is calculated as `false` if any [Bypass Actors](https://docs.github.com/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/creating-rulesets-for-a-repository#granting-bypass-permissions-for-your-ruleset)

 are defined on any rule, regardless of if they are admins.



Different types of branch protection protect against different risks:



  - Require code review: 

    - requires at least one reviewer, which greatly

    reduces the risk that a compromised contributor can inject malicious code.

    Review also increases the likelihood that an unintentional vulnerability in

    a contribution will be detected and fixed before the change is accepted.



    - requiring two or more reviewers protects even more from the insider risk 

    whereby a compromised contributor can be used by an attacker to LGTM 

    the attacker PR and inject a malicious code as if it was legit.



  - Prevent force push: prevents use of the `--force` command on public

    branches, which overwrites code irrevocably. This protection prevents the

    rewriting of public history without external notice.



  - Require [status checks](https://docs.github.com/en/github/collaborating-with-pull-requests/collaborating-on-repositories-with-code-quality-features/about-status-checks):

    ensures that all required CI tests are met before a change is accepted.



Although requiring code review can greatly reduce the chance that

unintentional or malicious code enters the "main" branch, it is not feasible for

all projects, such as those that don't have many active participants. For more

discussion, see [Code Reviews](https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/blob/main/docs/checks.md#code-review).



Additionally, in some cases these rules will need to be suspended. For example,

if a past commit includes illegal content such as child pornography, it may be

necessary to use a force push to rewrite the history rather than simply hide the

commit.



This test has tiered scoring. Each tier must be fully satisfied to achieve points at the next tier. For example, if you fulfill the Tier 3 checks but do not fulfill all the Tier 2 checks, you will not receive any points for Tier 3.



Note: If Scorecard is run without an administrative access token, the requirements that specify “For administrators” can be safely ignored, and scores will be determined as if all such requirements have been met.



Tier 1 Requirements (3/10 points):

  - Prevent force push

  - Prevent branch deletion



Tier 2 Requirements (6/10 points):

  - Require at least 1 reviewer for approval before merging (for administrators, this requirement weights twice than the others in this tier)

  - For administrators: Require PRs prior to make any code changes

  - For administrators: Require branch to be up to date before merging

  - For administrators: Require approval of the most recent reviewable push



Tier 3 Requirements (8/10 points):

  - Require branch to pass at least 1 status check before merging



Tier 4 Requirements (9/10 points):

  - Require at least 2 reviewers for approval before merging

  - Require review from code owners



Tier 5 Requirements (10/10 points):

  - For administrators: Dismiss stale reviews and approvals when new commits are pushed

  - For administrators: Include administrator for review



GitLab Integration Status:

  - GitLab associates releases with commits and not with the branch. Releases are ignored in this portion of the scoring.

- If your project is producing an application, declare all your dependencies with specific versions in your package format file (e.g. `package.json` for npm, `requirements.txt` for python, `packages.config` for nuget). For C/C++, check in the code from a trusted source and add a `README` on the specific version used (and the archive SHA hashes).

- If your project is producing an application and the package manager supports lock files (e.g. `package-lock.json` for npm), make sure to check these in the source code as well. These files maintain signatures for the entire dependency tree and saves from future exploitation in case the package is compromised.

- For Dockerfiles used in building and releasing your project, pin dependencies by hash. See [Dockerfile](https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/blob/main/cron/internal/worker/Dockerfile) for example. If you are using a manifest list to support builds across multiple architectures, you can pin to the manifest list hash instead of a single image hash. You can use a tool like [crane](https://github.com/google/go-containerregistry/blob/main/cmd/crane/README.md) to obtain the hash of the manifest list like in this [example](https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/issues/1773#issuecomment-1076699039).

- For GitHub workflows used in building and releasing your project, pin dependencies by hash. See [main.yaml](https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/blob/f55b86d6627cc3717e3a0395e03305e81b9a09be/.github/workflows/main.yml#L27) for example. To determine the permissions needed for your workflows, you may use [StepSecurity's online tool](https://app.stepsecurity.io/secureworkflow/) by ticking the "Pin actions to a full length commit SHA". You may also tick the "Restrict permissions for GITHUB_TOKEN" to fix issues found by the Token-Permissions check.

- To help update your dependencies after pinning them, use tools such as those listed for the dependency update tool check.



**Severity**: Medium



**Details**:

Risk: `Medium` (possible compromised dependencies)



This check tries to determine if the project pins dependencies used during its build and release process.

A "pinned dependency" is a dependency that is explicitly set to a specific hash instead of

allowing a mutable version or range of versions. It

is currently limited to repositories hosted on GitHub, and does not support

other source hosting repositories (i.e., Forges).



The check works by looking for unpinned dependencies in Dockerfiles, shell scripts, and GitHub workflows

which are used during the build and release process of a project.

Special considerations for Go modules treat full semantic versions as pinned

due to how the Go tool verifies downloaded content against the hashes when anyone first downloaded the module.



Pinned dependencies reduce several security risks:



  - They ensure that checking and deployment are all done with the same

    software, reducing deployment risks, simplifying debugging, and enabling

    reproducibility.

  - They can help mitigate compromised dependencies from undermining the

    security of the project (in the case where you've evaluated the pinned

    dependency, you are confident it's not compromised, and a later version is

    released that is compromised).

  - They are one way to [counter dependency confusion (aka substitution) attacks](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/resources/3-ways-to-mitigate-risk-using-private-package-feeds/),

    in which an application uses multiple feeds to acquire software packages (a

    "hybrid configuration"), and attackers fool the user into using a malicious

    package via a feed that was not expected for that package.



However, pinning dependencies can inhibit software updates, either because of a

security vulnerability or because the pinned version is compromised. Mitigate

this risk by:



  - using automated tools to notify applications when their dependencies are

    outdated;

  - quickly updating applications that do pin dependencies.



For projects hosted on GitHub, you can learn more about

dependencies using the [GitHub dependency graph](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/supply-chain-security/understanding-your-software-supply-chain/about-the-dependency-graph).

","id":"PinnedDependenciesID","name":"Pinned-Dependencies","security_severity_level":"medium"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:23Z"}

    in which an application uses multiple feeds to acquire software packages (a

    "hybrid configuration"), and attackers fool the user into using a malicious

    package via a feed that was not expected for that package.



However, pinning dependencies can inhibit software updates, either because of a

security vulnerability or because the pinned version is compromised. Mitigate

this risk by:



  - using automated tools to notify applications when their dependencies are

    outdated;

  - quickly updating applications that do pin dependencies.



For projects hosted on GitHub, you can learn more about

dependencies using the [GitHub dependency graph](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/supply-chain-security/understanding-your-software-supply-chain/about-the-dependency-graph).

Click Remediation section below to solve this issue","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":49,"rule":{"description":"Code-Review","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- If the project has only one contributor, or does not have enough reviewers to practically require that all contributions be reviewed, try to recruit more maintainers to the project who will be willing to review others' work. Ideally at least some of these people will be from different organizations (see [Contributors](checks.md#contributors)). If the project has very limited utility, consider expanding its intended utility so more people will be interested in improving it, and make that larger scope clear to potential contributors.

- Follow security best practices by performing strict code reviews for every new pull request / merge request.

- Make "code reviews" mandatory in your repository configuration. ([Instructions for GitHub.](https://docs.github.com/en/github/administering-a-repository/about-protected-branches#require-pull-request-reviews-before-merging))

- Enforce the rule for administrators / code owners as well. ([Instructions for GitHub.](https://docs.github.com/en/github/administering-a-repository/about-protected-branches#include-administrators))



**Severity**: High



**Details**:

Risk: `High` (unintentional vulnerabilities or possible injection of malicious

code)



This check determines whether the project requires human code review

before pull requests (merge requests) are merged.



Reviews detect various unintentional problems, including vulnerabilities that

can be fixed immediately before they are merged, which improves the quality of

the code. Reviews may also detect or deter an attacker trying to insert

malicious code (either as a malicious contributor or as an attacker who has

subverted a contributor's account), because a reviewer might detect the

subversion.



The check determines whether the most recent changes (over the last ~30 commits) have 

an approval on GitHub

or if the merger is different from the committer (implicit review). It also

performs a similar check for reviews using

[Prow](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/tree/master/prow#readme) (labels

"lgtm" or "approved") and [Gerrit](https://www.gerritcodereview.com/) ("Reviewed-on" and "Reviewed-by").

If recent changes are solely bot activity (e.g. Dependabot, Renovate bot, or custom bots),

the check returns inconclusively.



Scoring is leveled instead of proportional to make the check more predictable.

If any bot-originated changes are unreviewed, 3 points are deducted. If any human

changes are unreviewed, 7 points are deducted if a single change is unreviewed, and

another 3 are deducted if multiple changes are unreviewed.



Review by bots, including bots powered by

artificial intelligence / machine learning (AI/ML),

do not count as code review.

Such reviews do not provide confidence that there will

be a second person who understands the

code change (e.g., if the originator suddenly becomes unavailable).

However, analysis by bots

may be able to meet (at least in part) the [SAST](#sast) criterion.



Note: Requiring reviews for all changes is infeasible for some projects, such as

those with only one active participant. Even a project with multiple active

contributors may not have enough active participation to be able to require

review of all proposed changes. Projects with a small number of active

participants instead sometimes aim for a review of a

percentage of proposals (e.g., "at least half of all proposed changes are

reviewed").



Requiring review does not eliminate all risks. The other reviewers might fail to

notice unintentional vulnerabilities or malicious code, be colluding with a

malicious developer, or even be the same person (using a "[sock

puppet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sock_puppet_account)" account).

","id":"CodeReviewID","name":"Code-Review","security_severity_level":"high"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z"}

{"created_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z","html_url":"https://github.com/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/security/code-scanning/50","most_recent_instance":{"analysis_key":".github/workflows/scorecard.yml:analysis","category":"supply-chain/online-scm","location":{"end_column":0,"end_line":1,"path":"no file associated with this alert","start_column":1,"start_line":1},"message":"score is 0: no effort to earn an OpenSSF best practices badge detected
Click Remediation section below to solve this issue","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":50,"rule":{"description":"CII-Best-Practices","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- Sign up for the [OpenSSF Best Practices program](https://www.bestpractices.dev/).



**Severity**: Low



**Details**:

Risk: `Low` (possibly not following security best practices)



This check determines whether the project has earned an [OpenSSF (formerly CII) Best Practices Badge](https://www.bestpractices.dev/) at the passing, silver, or gold level.

The OpenSSF Best Practices badge indicates whether or not that the project uses a set of security-focused best development practices for open

source software. The check uses the URL for the Git repo and the OpenSSF Best Practices badge API.



The OpenSSF Best Practices badge has 3 tiers: passing, silver, and gold. We give

full credit to projects that meet the [gold criteria](https://www.bestpractices.dev/criteria/2), which is a significant achievement for projects and requires multiple developers in the project.

Lower scores represent a project that has met the silver criteria, met the passing criteria, or is working to achieve the passing badge, with increasingly more points awarded as more criteria are met. Note that even meeting the passing criteria is a significant achievement.



- [gold badge](https://www.bestpractices.dev/criteria/2): 10

- [silver badge](https://www.bestpractices.dev/criteria/1): 7

- [passing badge](https://www.bestpractices.dev/criteria/0): 5

- in progress badge: 2



Some of these criteria overlap with other Scorecard checks.

However, note that in those overlapping cases, Scorecard can only report what it can automatically detect, while the OpenSSF Best Practices badge can report on claims and claim justifications from people (this counters false negatives and positives but has the challenge of requiring additional work from people).

","id":"CIIBestPracticesID","name":"CII-Best-Practices","security_severity_level":"low"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z"}

Warn: no fuzzer integrations found
Click Remediation section below to solve this issue","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":51,"rule":{"description":"Fuzzing","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- Integrate the project with OSS-Fuzz by following the instructions [here](https://google.github.io/oss-fuzz/).



**Severity**: Medium



**Details**:

Risk: `Medium` (possible vulnerabilities in code)



This check tries to determine if the project uses

[fuzzing](https://owasp.org/www-community/Fuzzing) by checking:

1. if the repository name is included in the [OSS-Fuzz](https://github.com/google/oss-fuzz) project list;

2. if [ClusterFuzzLite](https://google.github.io/clusterfuzzlite/) is deployed in the repository;

3. if there are user-defined language-specified fuzzing functions in the repository.

   - currently only supports [Go fuzzing](https://go.dev/doc/fuzz/),

   - a limited set of property-based testing libraries for Haskell including [QuickCheck](https://hackage.haskell.org/package/QuickCheck), [Hedgehog](https://hedgehog.qa/), [validity](https://hackage.haskell.org/package/validity) or [SmallCheck](https://hackage.haskell.org/package/smallcheck),

   - a limited set of property-based testing libraries for JavaScript and TypeScript including [fast-check](https://fast-check.dev/).

   - a limited set of property-based testing libraries for Erlang, including proper and quickcheck.



Fuzzing, or fuzz testing, is the practice of feeding unexpected or random data

into a program to expose bugs. Regular fuzzing is important to detect

vulnerabilities that may be exploited by others, especially since attackers can

also use fuzzing to find the same flaws.



Note: A project that fulfills this criterion with other tools may still receive

a low score on this test. There are many ways to implement fuzzing, and it is

challenging for an automated tool like Scorecard to detect them all. A low score

is therefore not a definitive indication that the project is at risk.

","id":"FuzzingID","name":"Fuzzing","security_severity_level":"medium"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z"}

{"created_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:22Z","html_url":"https://github.com/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/security/code-scanning/42","most_recent_instance":{"analysis_key":".github/workflows/scorecard.yml:analysis","category":"supply-chain/branch-protection","location":{"end_column":0,"end_line":1,"path":"no file associated with this alert","start_column":1,"start_line":1},"message":"score is 3: branch protection is not maximal on development and all release branches:
Warn: branch 'main' does not require approvers
Warn: codeowners review is required - but no codeowners file found in repo
Warn: 'last push approval' is disabled on branch 'main'
Warn: 'up-to-date branches' is disabled on branch 'main'
Click Remediation section below to solve this issue","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":42,"rule":{"description":"Branch-Protection","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- Enable branch protection settings in your source hosting provider to avoid force pushes or deletion of your important branches.

- For GitHub, check out the steps for rulesets [here](https://docs.github.com/en/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/creating-rulesets-for-a-repository) and rules [here](https://docs.github.com/en/github/administering-a-repository/managing-a-branch-protection-rule).



**Severity**: High



**Details**:

Risk: `High` (vulnerable to intentional malicious code injection)



This check determines whether a project's default and release branches are

protected with GitHub's [branch protection](https://docs.github.com/github/administering-a-repository/defining-the-mergeability-of-pull-requests/about-protected-branches) 

or [repository rules](https://docs.github.com/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/about-rulesets) settings.

Branch protection allows maintainers to define rules that enforce

certain workflows for branches, such as requiring review or passing certain

status checks before acceptance into a main branch, or preventing rewriting of

public history.



Note: The following settings queried by the Branch-Protection check require an admin token: `DismissStaleReviews`, `EnforceAdmins`, `RequireLastPushApproval`, `RequiresStatusChecks` and `UpToDateBeforeMerge`. If

the provided token does not have admin access, the check will query the branch

settings accessible to non-admins and provide results based only on these settings.

However, all of these settings are accessible via Repo Rules. `EnforceAdmins` is calculated slightly differently.

This setting is calculated as `false` if any [Bypass Actors](https://docs.github.com/repositories/configuring-branches-and-merges-in-your-repository/managing-rulesets/creating-rulesets-for-a-repository#granting-bypass-permissions-for-your-ruleset)

 are defined on any rule, regardless of if they are admins.



Different types of branch protection protect against different risks:



  - Require code review: 

    - requires at least one reviewer, which greatly

    reduces the risk that a compromised contributor can inject malicious code.

    Review also increases the likelihood that an unintentional vulnerability in

    a contribution will be detected and fixed before the change is accepted.



    - requiring two or more reviewers protects even more from the insider risk 

    whereby a compromised contributor can be used by an attacker to LGTM 

    the attacker PR and inject a malicious code as if it was legit.



  - Prevent force push: prevents use of the `--force` command on public

    branches, which overwrites code irrevocably. This protection prevents the

    rewriting of public history without external notice.



  - Require [status checks](https://docs.github.com/en/github/collaborating-with-pull-requests/collaborating-on-repositories-with-code-quality-features/about-status-checks):

    ensures that all required CI tests are met before a change is accepted.



Although requiring code review can greatly reduce the chance that

unintentional or malicious code enters the "main" branch, it is not feasible for

all projects, such as those that don't have many active participants. For more

discussion, see [Code Reviews](https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/blob/main/docs/checks.md#code-review).



Additionally, in some cases these rules will need to be suspended. For example,

if a past commit includes illegal content such as child pornography, it may be

necessary to use a force push to rewrite the history rather than simply hide the

commit.



This test has tiered scoring. Each tier must be fully satisfied to achieve points at the next tier. For example, if you fulfill the Tier 3 checks but do not fulfill all the Tier 2 checks, you will not receive any points for Tier 3.



Note: If Scorecard is run without an administrative access token, the requirements that specify “For administrators” can be safely ignored, and scores will be determined as if all such requirements have been met.



Tier 1 Requirements (3/10 points):

  - Prevent force push

  - Prevent branch deletion



Tier 2 Requirements (6/10 points):

  - Require at least 1 reviewer for approval before merging (for administrators, this requirement weights twice than the others in this tier)

  - For administrators: Require PRs prior to make any code changes

  - For administrators: Require branch to be up to date before merging

  - For administrators: Require approval of the most recent reviewable push



Tier 3 Requirements (8/10 points):

  - Require branch to pass at least 1 status check before merging



Tier 4 Requirements (9/10 points):

  - Require at least 2 reviewers for approval before merging

  - Require review from code owners



Tier 5 Requirements (10/10 points):

  - For administrators: Dismiss stale reviews and approvals when new commits are pushed

  - For administrators: Include administrator for review



GitLab Integration Status:

  - GitLab associates releases with commits and not with the branch. Releases are ignored in this portion of the scoring.

","id":"BranchProtectionID","name":"Branch-Protection","security_severity_level":"high"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:22Z"}

Click Remediation section below for further remediation help","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":43,"rule":{"description":"Pinned-Dependencies","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- If your project is producing an application, declare all your dependencies with specific versions in your package format file (e.g. `package.json` for npm, `requirements.txt` for python, `packages.config` for nuget). For C/C++, check in the code from a trusted source and add a `README` on the specific version used (and the archive SHA hashes).

- If your project is producing an application and the package manager supports lock files (e.g. `package-lock.json` for npm), make sure to check these in the source code as well. These files maintain signatures for the entire dependency tree and saves from future exploitation in case the package is compromised.

- For Dockerfiles used in building and releasing your project, pin dependencies by hash. See [Dockerfile](https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/blob/main/cron/internal/worker/Dockerfile) for example. If you are using a manifest list to support builds across multiple architectures, you can pin to the manifest list hash instead of a single image hash. You can use a tool like [crane](https://github.com/google/go-containerregistry/blob/main/cmd/crane/README.md) to obtain the hash of the manifest list like in this [example](https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/issues/1773#issuecomment-1076699039).

- For GitHub workflows used in building and releasing your project, pin dependencies by hash. See [main.yaml](https://github.com/ossf/scorecard/blob/f55b86d6627cc3717e3a0395e03305e81b9a09be/.github/workflows/main.yml#L27) for example. To determine the permissions needed for your workflows, you may use [StepSecurity's online tool](https://app.stepsecurity.io/secureworkflow/) by ticking the "Pin actions to a full length commit SHA". You may also tick the "Restrict permissions for GITHUB_TOKEN" to fix issues found by the Token-Permissions check.

- To help update your dependencies after pinning them, use tools such as those listed for the dependency update tool check.



**Severity**: Medium



**Details**:

Risk: `Medium` (possible compromised dependencies)



This check tries to determine if the project pins dependencies used during its build and release process.

A "pinned dependency" is a dependency that is explicitly set to a specific hash instead of

allowing a mutable version or range of versions. It

is currently limited to repositories hosted on GitHub, and does not support

other source hosting repositories (i.e., Forges).



The check works by looking for unpinned dependencies in Dockerfiles, shell scripts, and GitHub workflows

which are used during the build and release process of a project.

Special considerations for Go modules treat full semantic versions as pinned

due to how the Go tool verifies downloaded content against the hashes when anyone first downloaded the module.



Pinned dependencies reduce several security risks:



  - They ensure that checking and deployment are all done with the same

    software, reducing deployment risks, simplifying debugging, and enabling

    reproducibility.

  - They can help mitigate compromised dependencies from undermining the

    security of the project (in the case where you've evaluated the pinned

    dependency, you are confident it's not compromised, and a later version is

    released that is compromised).

  - They are one way to [counter dependency confusion (aka substitution) attacks](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/resources/3-ways-to-mitigate-risk-using-private-package-feeds/),

    in which an application uses multiple feeds to acquire software packages (a

    "hybrid configuration"), and attackers fool the user into using a malicious

    package via a feed that was not expected for that package.



However, pinning dependencies can inhibit software updates, either because of a

security vulnerability or because the pinned version is compromised. Mitigate

this risk by:



  - using automated tools to notify applications when their dependencies are

    outdated;

  - quickly updating applications that do pin dependencies.



For projects hosted on GitHub, you can learn more about

dependencies using the [GitHub dependency graph](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/supply-chain-security/understanding-your-software-supply-chain/about-the-dependency-graph).

","id":"PinnedDependenciesID","name":"Pinned-Dependencies","security_severity_level":"medium"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:23Z"}

Pinned dependencies reduce several security risks:



  - They ensure that checking and deployment are all done with the same

    software, reducing deployment risks, simplifying debugging, and enabling

    reproducibility.

  - They can help mitigate compromised dependencies from undermining the

    security of the project (in the case where you've evaluated the pinned

    dependency, you are confident it's not compromised, and a later version is

    released that is compromised).

  - They are one way to [counter dependency confusion (aka substitution) attacks](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/resources/3-ways-to-mitigate-risk-using-private-package-feeds/),

    in which an application uses multiple feeds to acquire software packages (a

    "hybrid configuration"), and attackers fool the user into using a malicious

    package via a feed that was not expected for that package.



However, pinning dependencies can inhibit software updates, either because of a

security vulnerability or because the pinned version is compromised. Mitigate

this risk by:



  - using automated tools to notify applications when their dependencies are

    outdated;

  - quickly updating applications that do pin dependencies.



For projects hosted on GitHub, you can learn more about

dependencies using the [GitHub dependency graph](https://docs.github.com/en/code-security/supply-chain-security/understanding-your-software-supply-chain/about-the-dependency-graph).

","id":"PinnedDependenciesID","name":"Pinned-Dependencies","security_severity_level":"medium"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:23Z"}

{"created_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z","html_url":"https://github.com/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/security/code-scanning/49","most_recent_instance":{"analysis_key":".github/workflows/scorecard.yml:analysis","category":"supply-chain/online-scm","location":{"end_column":0,"end_line":1,"path":"no file associated with this alert","start_column":1,"start_line":1},"message":"score is 0: Found 0/4 approved changesets -- score normalized to 0
Click Remediation section below to solve this issue","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":49,"rule":{"description":"Code-Review","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- If the project has only one contributor, or does not have enough reviewers to practically require that all contributions be reviewed, try to recruit more maintainers to the project who will be willing to review others' work. Ideally at least some of these people will be from different organizations (see [Contributors](checks.md#contributors)). If the project has very limited utility, consider expanding its intended utility so more people will be interested in improving it, and make that larger scope clear to potential contributors.

- Follow security best practices by performing strict code reviews for every new pull request / merge request.

- Make "code reviews" mandatory in your repository configuration. ([Instructions for GitHub.](https://docs.github.com/en/github/administering-a-repository/about-protected-branches#require-pull-request-reviews-before-merging))

- Enforce the rule for administrators / code owners as well. ([Instructions for GitHub.](https://docs.github.com/en/github/administering-a-repository/about-protected-branches#include-administrators))



**Severity**: High



**Details**:

Risk: `High` (unintentional vulnerabilities or possible injection of malicious

code)



This check determines whether the project requires human code review

before pull requests (merge requests) are merged.



Reviews detect various unintentional problems, including vulnerabilities that

can be fixed immediately before they are merged, which improves the quality of

the code. Reviews may also detect or deter an attacker trying to insert

malicious code (either as a malicious contributor or as an attacker who has

subverted a contributor's account), because a reviewer might detect the

subversion.



The check determines whether the most recent changes (over the last ~30 commits) have 

an approval on GitHub

or if the merger is different from the committer (implicit review). It also

performs a similar check for reviews using

[Prow](https://github.com/kubernetes/test-infra/tree/master/prow#readme) (labels

"lgtm" or "approved") and [Gerrit](https://www.gerritcodereview.com/) ("Reviewed-on" and "Reviewed-by").

If recent changes are solely bot activity (e.g. Dependabot, Renovate bot, or custom bots),

the check returns inconclusively.



Scoring is leveled instead of proportional to make the check more predictable.

If any bot-originated changes are unreviewed, 3 points are deducted. If any human

changes are unreviewed, 7 points are deducted if a single change is unreviewed, and

another 3 are deducted if multiple changes are unreviewed.



Review by bots, including bots powered by

artificial intelligence / machine learning (AI/ML),

do not count as code review.

Such reviews do not provide confidence that there will

be a second person who understands the

code change (e.g., if the originator suddenly becomes unavailable).

However, analysis by bots

may be able to meet (at least in part) the [SAST](#sast) criterion.



Note: Requiring reviews for all changes is infeasible for some projects, such as

those with only one active participant. Even a project with multiple active

contributors may not have enough active participation to be able to require

review of all proposed changes. Projects with a small number of active

participants instead sometimes aim for a review of a

percentage of proposals (e.g., "at least half of all proposed changes are

reviewed").



Requiring review does not eliminate all risks. The other reviewers might fail to

notice unintentional vulnerabilities or malicious code, be colluding with a

malicious developer, or even be the same person (using a "[sock

puppet](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sock_puppet_account)" account).

","id":"CodeReviewID","name":"Code-Review","security_severity_level":"high"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z"}
