{"created_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z","html_url":"https://github.com/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/security/code-scanning/50","most_recent_instance":{"analysis_key":".github/workflows/scorecard.yml:analysis","category":"supply-chain/online-scm","location":{"end_column":0,"end_line":1,"path":"no file associated with this alert","start_column":1,"start_line":1},"message":"score is 0: no effort to earn an OpenSSF best practices badge detected
Click Remediation section below to solve this issue","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":50,"rule":{"description":"CII-Best-Practices","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- Sign up for the [OpenSSF Best Practices program](https://www.bestpractices.dev/).



**Severity**: Low



**Details**:

Risk: `Low` (possibly not following security best practices)



This check determines whether the project has earned an [OpenSSF (formerly CII) Best Practices Badge](https://www.bestpractices.dev/) at the passing, silver, or gold level.

The OpenSSF Best Practices badge indicates whether or not that the project uses a set of security-focused best development practices for open

source software. The check uses the URL for the Git repo and the OpenSSF Best Practices badge API.



The OpenSSF Best Practices badge has 3 tiers: passing, silver, and gold. We give

full credit to projects that meet the [gold criteria](https://www.bestpractices.dev/criteria/2), which is a significant achievement for projects and requires multiple developers in the project.

Lower scores represent a project that has met the silver criteria, met the passing criteria, or is working to achieve the passing badge, with increasingly more points awarded as more criteria are met. Note that even meeting the passing criteria is a significant achievement.



- [gold badge](https://www.bestpractices.dev/criteria/2): 10

- [silver badge](https://www.bestpractices.dev/criteria/1): 7

- [passing badge](https://www.bestpractices.dev/criteria/0): 5

- in progress badge: 2



Some of these criteria overlap with other Scorecard checks.

However, note that in those overlapping cases, Scorecard can only report what it can automatically detect, while the OpenSSF Best Practices badge can report on claims and claim justifications from people (this counters false negatives and positives but has the challenge of requiring additional work from people).

","id":"CIIBestPracticesID","name":"CII-Best-Practices","security_severity_level":"low"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z"}
--- #51 ---
{"created_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z","html_url":"https://github.com/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/security/code-scanning/51","most_recent_instance":{"analysis_key":".github/workflows/scorecard.yml:analysis","category":"supply-chain/online-scm","location":{"end_column":0,"end_line":1,"path":"no file associated with this alert","start_column":1,"start_line":1},"message":"score is 0: project is not fuzzed:
Warn: no fuzzer integrations found
Click Remediation section below to solve this issue","ref":"refs/heads/main"},"number":51,"rule":{"description":"Fuzzing","help":"**Remediation (click "Show more" below)**:

- Integrate the project with OSS-Fuzz by following the instructions [here](https://google.github.io/oss-fuzz/).



**Severity**: Medium



**Details**:

Risk: `Medium` (possible vulnerabilities in code)



This check tries to determine if the project uses

[fuzzing](https://owasp.org/www-community/Fuzzing) by checking:

1. if the repository name is included in the [OSS-Fuzz](https://github.com/google/oss-fuzz) project list;

2. if [ClusterFuzzLite](https://google.github.io/clusterfuzzlite/) is deployed in the repository;

3. if there are user-defined language-specified fuzzing functions in the repository.

   - currently only supports [Go fuzzing](https://go.dev/doc/fuzz/),

   - a limited set of property-based testing libraries for Haskell including [QuickCheck](https://hackage.haskell.org/package/QuickCheck), [Hedgehog](https://hedgehog.qa/), [validity](https://hackage.haskell.org/package/validity) or [SmallCheck](https://hackage.haskell.org/package/smallcheck),

   - a limited set of property-based testing libraries for JavaScript and TypeScript including [fast-check](https://fast-check.dev/).

   - a limited set of property-based testing libraries for Erlang, including proper and quickcheck.



Fuzzing, or fuzz testing, is the practice of feeding unexpected or random data

into a program to expose bugs. Regular fuzzing is important to detect

vulnerabilities that may be exploited by others, especially since attackers can

also use fuzzing to find the same flaws.



Note: A project that fulfills this criterion with other tools may still receive

a low score on this test. There are many ways to implement fuzzing, and it is

challenging for an automated tool like Scorecard to detect them all. A low score

is therefore not a definitive indication that the project is at risk.

","id":"FuzzingID","name":"Fuzzing","security_severity_level":"medium"},"state":"open","tool":"Scorecard","updated_at":"2026-07-19T16:01:24Z"}

arguments.command:
printf '%s
' '--- full mainline ruleset ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/rulesets/9651727' 2>&1 | jq '{id,name,target,enforcement,conditions,rules,bypass_actors}'; printf '%s
' '--- collaborators (permission-gated) ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/collaborators?affiliation=direct&per_page=100' --paginate --jq '.[] | {login,permissions}' 2>&1 || true; printf '%s
' '--- protected branch required signatures endpoint ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/branches/main/protection/required_signatures' 2>&1 || true; printf '%s
' '--- branch protection restrictions endpoint ---'; gh api 'repos/Scetrov/evefrontier-rs/branches/main/protection/restrictions' 2>&1 || true

printf '%s
' '--- compose service build references ---'; grep -nE -C 3 'build:|Dockerfile|evefrontier-service' docker-compose.yml; printf '%s
' '--- Dockerfile tracked history ---'; git log --format='%h %ad %an %s' --date=short -- crates/evefrontier-service-route/Dockerfile crates/evefrontier-service-scout-gates/Dockerfile crates/evefrontier-service-scout-range/Dockerfile; printf '%s
' '--- only Docker FROM instructions (outside agent artifacts) ---'; find . -path './.git' -prune -o -path './.pi*' -prune -o -iname 'Dockerfile*' -type f -print -exec grep -nE '^FROM ' {} \;

printf '%s
' '--- test/fuzz dependencies and configurations, excluding agent output ---'; grep -RInE --exclude-dir=.git --exclude-dir=.pi --exclude-dir=.pi-subagents --exclude-dir=node_modules --exclude-dir=target --exclude='Cargo.lock' 'proptest|quickcheck|arbitrary|cargo[- ]fuzz|libfuzzer|fuzzing|fuzzer|honggfuzz|afl|oss-fuzz|clusterfuzz' Cargo.toml crates .github docs Makefile package.json nx.json 2>/dev/null || true; printf '%s
' '--- untrusted-input boundaries ---'; grep -RInE --exclude-dir=.git --exclude-dir=.pi --exclude-dir=.pi-subagents 'ZipArchive|from_reader|from_slice|from_str|Json<|Query<|deserialize|read_to_end' crates --include='*.rs' | head -100; printf '%s
' '--- selected parser source locations ---'; nl -ba crates/evefrontier-lib/src/fmap.rs | sed -n '1,260p';

printf '%s
' '--- status / staged state (before artifact write) ---'; git status --short; printf '%s
' '--- staged diff ---'; git diff --cached --name-only; printf '%s
' '--- working diff source tracked ---'; git diff --name-only; printf '%s
' '--- ignore artifact directory ---'; git check-ignore -v .pi-subagents/artifacts/outputs/26f17981-1a1f-46f8-94a5-83d3ece47a09/scorecard-independent-review.md || true

--- compose service build references ---
42-
43-  # Route planning service
44-  route:
45:    build:
46-      context: .
47:      dockerfile: crates/evefrontier-service-route/Dockerfile
48-    environment:
49-      - EVEFRONTIER_DATA_PATH=/data/static_data.db
50-      - RUST_LOG=info
--
56-
57-  # Gate neighbor lookup service
58-  scout-gates:
59:    build:
60-      context: .
61:      dockerfile: crates/evefrontier-service-scout-gates/Dockerfile
62-    environment:
63-      - EVEFRONTIER_DATA_PATH=/data/static_data.db
64-      - RUST_LOG=info
--
70-
71-  # Spatial range search service
72-  scout-range:
73:    build:
74-      context: .
75:      dockerfile: crates/evefrontier-service-scout-range/Dockerfile
76-    environment:
77-      - EVEFRONTIER_DATA_PATH=/data/static_data.db
78-      - RUST_LOG=info
--- Dockerfile tracked history ---
281a65a 2026-07-11 Scetrov fix: remediate RUSTSEC-2026-0204 (#193)
f25ca21 2026-05-31 dependabot[bot] chore(deps): bump the cargo-dependencies group across 1 directory with 8 updates (#176)
855c4e2 2026-02-03 Scetrov chore: bump Rust toolchain to 1.93.0 (#72)
be75747 2025-12-30 Scetrov feat: Docker microservices and Kubernetes deployment support (#34)
--- only Docker FROM instructions (outside agent artifacts) ---
./crates/evefrontier-service-route/Dockerfile
2:FROM rust:1.97.0-bookworm AS builder
27:FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12:nonroot
./crates/evefrontier-service-scout-range/Dockerfile

text:
1	# Threat model
     2	
     3	## Scope
     4	
     5	This document covers the repository's Rust route-planning library, CLI, Lambda functions,
     6	Kubernetes services, and WASM modules. The system calculates routes and scouting paths from
     7	EVE Frontier data, including temperature and fuel-aware route information. It does not replace
     8	the vulnerability-reporting process in [SECURITY.md](../SECURITY.md).
     9	
    10	## Assets and security objectives
    11	
    12	- Source code, dependency lockfiles, CI/CD workflow definitions, and release configuration must
    13	  be protected from unauthorized modification.
    14	- Route and scouting inputs, downloaded data, and generated outputs must retain integrity so that
    15	  users can make decisions from trustworthy results.
    16	- Published container images, binaries, SBOMs, scans, and signatures must be traceable to the
    17	  reviewed build that produced them.
    18	- Repository and CI credentials, including GitHub tokens and configured secrets, must not be
    19	  exposed or granted more access than the task requires.
    20	
    21	## Trust boundaries and entry points
    22	
    23	- CLI arguments, configuration, datasets, and downloaded input files cross from users or external
    24	  data sources into route calculation and parsing code.
    25	- Lambda, Kubernetes, and WASM deployment interfaces cross from callers and hosting platforms into
    26	  service code; deployment-specific authentication, authorization, and network controls are owned
    27	  by the environment and must be reviewed before deployment.
    28	- Pull requests, workflow-dispatch inputs, tags, dependencies, and GitHub Actions cross into CI/CD.
    29	  They are untrusted until validation and review complete.
    30	- OCI registries and release consumers are external to the repository. Image digests, signatures,
    31	  SBOMs, and provenance are the integrity evidence at this boundary.
    32	
    33	## Primary threats and mitigations
    34	
    35	### Untrusted route, scouting, or dataset input
    36	
    37	Validate and bound inputs in the relevant parser and service entry point, and add regression tests
    38	for rejected input. Maintainers must define deployment-specific request limits and monitoring.
    39	
    40	### Compromised dependencies or mutable CI actions
    41	
    42	Lock dependencies, review dependency changes, pin Actions to reviewed commits, and use Dependabot
    43	updates. Administrators must enable dependency alerts and require dependency-review checks.
    44	
    45	### Unreviewed workflow or release changes
    46	
    47	Require PR review, protect workflow and release paths, use least-privilege tokens, and record build
    48	provenance. Administrators must enforce branch protection, CODEOWNERS, and release permissions.
    49	
    50	### Unsafe workflow-dispatch input
    51	
    52	Pass dispatch values through step environment variables, quote shell expansion, and accept only
    53	v-prefixed semantic versions. Maintainers must keep validation aligned with supported release-tag
    54	formats.
    55	
    56	### Forged or substituted OCI images
    57	
    58	Publish image provenance, retain SBOMs, scan images, and verify cosign signatures and digests
    59	before deployment. Release owners must verify registry-attached attestations and restrict
    60	publishing.
    61	
    62	### Secret exposure in source or CI logs
    63	
    64	Use GitHub secret expressions rather than hardcoded values and limit token permissions.
    65	Administrators must enable secret scanning and push protection.
    66	
    67	## Security-sensitive decisions
    68	
    69	- Docker releases use GitHub OIDC-backed cosign signing and now request Buildx provenance for
    70	  published images. Consumers should verify signatures, digests, and provenance before deployment.
    71	- CI workflows use explicit permissions and SHA-pinned third-party actions. Pin updates require
    72	  review of the upstream commit and its release notes.
    73	- The accepted DevSecOps practices in
    74	  [ADR 0007](adrs/0007-devsecops-practices.md) govern CI checks, dependency scanning, and release
    75	  integrity expectations.
    76	
    77	## Assumptions and review cadence
    78	
    79	This model deliberately does not assert deployment architecture, authentication, data
    80	classification, retention, or operational ownership that is not documented in the repository.
    81	Maintainers should review it when adding an external interface, changing release or credential
    82	flows, introducing a new data source, or at least annually. Deployment owners should record the
    83	applicable environment-specific controls alongside their deployment configuration.
--- documented container base-tag rationale ---
   155	**Only use this for:**
   156	
   157	- False positives
   158	- Vulnerabilities that don't apply to our usage
   159	- Temporarily while waiting for upstream fix
   160	
   161	**Always include:**
   162	
   163	- The advisory ID
   164	- A clear comment explaining why it's safe to ignore
   165	- A tracking issue or TODO if temporary
   166	
   167	#### Option C: Replace Dependency
   168	
   169	If the vulnerable crate is unmaintained or the fix is delayed:
   170	
   171	1. Search for alternative crates with similar functionality
   172	2. Evaluate security posture and maintenance status
   173	3. Update code to use the replacement
   174	4. Test thoroughly
   175	
   176	### 4. Verify Fix
   177	
   178	After remediation:
   179	
   180	```bash
   181	# Run audit
   182	make audit
   183	
   184	# Run full test suite
   185	make test
   186	
   187	# Test pre-commit hook
   188	git add .
   189	git commit -m "fix: update vulnerable dependency"
   190	```
   191	
   192	## Advisory Database Updates
   193	
   194	The RustSec advisory database is updated frequently. To get the latest advisories:
   195	
   196	```bash
   197	cargo audit fetch
   198	```
   199	
   200	This is automatically done in CI and pre-commit hooks.
   201	
   202	## False Positives
   203	
   204	Sometimes `cargo-audit` reports advisories that don't affect our usage. Examples:
   205	
   206	- **Denial of service** vulnerabilities in a library we only use at build time
   207	- **Unsoundness issues** in unsafe code we don't trigger
   208	- **Platform-specific** vulnerabilities on platforms we don't support
   209	
   210	In these cases:
   211	
   212	1. Document why the advisory doesn't apply (in a commit message or comment)
   213	2. Add the advisory to `audit.toml` ignore list
   214	3. Include a TODO to revisit when a proper fix is available
   215	4. Link to a tracking issue if long-term
   216	
   217	## Integration with Other Tools
   218	
   219	### cargo-deny
   220	
   221	For more advanced dependency policy enforcement, consider
   222	[`cargo-deny`](https://github.com/EmbarkStudios/cargo-deny):
   223	
   224	- License policy enforcement
   225	- Dependency ban lists
   226	- More granular advisory controls
   227	
   228	### Dependabot
   229	
   230	GitHub Dependabot can automatically:
   231	
   232	- Detect vulnerable dependencies
   233	- Open PRs with version updates
   234	- Keep dependencies current
   235	
--- workflow line refs ---
     1	name: Scorecard
     2	
     3	on:
     4	  branch_protection_rule:
     5	  schedule:
     6	    - cron: "17 14 * * 1"
     7	  push:
     8	    branches:
     9	      - main
    10	  workflow_dispatch:
    11	
    12	permissions: read-all
    13	
    14	jobs:
    15	  analysis:
    16	    name: Scorecard supply-chain security
    17	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    18	    permissions:
    19	      security-events: write
    20	      id-token: write
    21	      contents: read
    22	      actions: read
    23	    steps:
    24	      - name: Run OpenSSF Scorecard
    25	        uses: ossf/scorecard-action@4eaacf0543bb3f2c246792bd56e8cdeffafb205a # v2.4.3
    26	        with:
    27	          results_file: results.sarif
    28	          results_format: sarif
    29	          publish_results: true
    30	      - name: Upload Scorecard results
    31	        uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@7188fc363630916deb702c7fdcf4e481b751f97a # v4
    32	        with:
    33	          sarif_file: results.sarif
    34	          category: scorecard
     1	name: Dependency Review
     2	
     3	on:
     4	  pull_request:
     5	    branches:
     6	      - main
     7	
     8	permissions:
     9	  contents: read
    10	
    11	jobs:
    12	  dependency-review:
    13	    name: Dependency review
    14	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    15	    steps:
    16	      - name: Review dependency changes
    17	        uses: actions/dependency-review-action@2031cfc080254a8a887f58cffee85186f0e49e48 # v4.9.0

text:
9-
10-1. **Rust Dependency Scanning** (`cargo-audit`): Scans Cargo dependencies against the RustSec
11-   Advisory Database
12:2. **Container Image Scanning** (Trivy): Scans Docker images for OS-level and application
13-   vulnerabilities
14-
15-Both tools run automatically in CI pipelines and have specific policies for handling
--
237-
238----
239-
240:## Container Image Scanning (Trivy)
241-
242-In addition to Rust dependency scanning, we scan our Docker container images using
243-[Trivy](https://trivy.dev/) to detect vulnerabilities in:
244-
245:- Base image OS packages (Debian packages in distroless)
246-- Any binaries or libraries bundled in the image
247-- Misconfigurations in the container setup
248-
--
257-
258-### Policy: Ignoring Unfixed Vulnerabilities
259-
260:We configure Trivy with `ignore-unfixed: true`, meaning:
261-
262-- ✅ **Fail** on CRITICAL/HIGH vulnerabilities that have patches available
263-- ⚠️ **Warn** (but don't fail) on vulnerabilities without upstream fixes
264-
265-**Rationale:**
266-
267:1. **We use Google's distroless base image** (`gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12:nonroot`), which is
268-   actively maintained and receives security updates
269-2. **Unfixed vulnerabilities have no remediation path** — we cannot patch what upstream hasn't fixed
270-3. **Rust dependencies are scanned separately** by `cargo-audit`, so we're not missing coverage
--
275-When Trivy reports unfixed vulnerabilities:
276-
277-1. **Check the advisory** to understand the risk and affected component
278:2. **Monitor upstream** (Debian security tracker, distroless releases)
279-3. **When a fix is released**, our next build will automatically fail until we update
280-4. **Update the base image** by rebuilding containers (the Dockerfile pulls `:nonroot` tag)
281-
--
292-trivy image ghcr.io/scetrov/evefrontier-rs/evefrontier-service-route:0.1.0
293-
294-# Scan with same settings as CI
295:trivy image --severity CRITICAL,HIGH --ignore-unfixed \
296-  ghcr.io/scetrov/evefrontier-rs/evefrontier-service-route:0.1.0
297-
298-# Generate SARIF report

arguments.content:
## Review

### Scope and evidence

`plan.md` and `progress.md` were requested but are absent at the repository root (`ENOENT`). This review therefore used the current checkout and live GitHub API state. `gh` is authenticated and the repository is public.

The ten findings are **all currently open Scorecard code-scanning alerts** (#42–#51), created 2026-07-19 16:01 UTC. The scheduled Scorecard run at 2026-07-20 14:59 UTC completed successfully against `main` commit `33e8b9161c0bddc1449f169370f3a8de6cbe48e4`; successful analysis does not mean its SARIF findings are resolved.

### Per-alert classification

| Alert | Scorecard severity | Classification | Verified evidence and disposition |
|---|---:|---|---|
| #42 Branch-Protection | High | **Accepted single-maintainer limitation** | Live API ruleset `mainline` is active on the default branch, prevents deletion and non-fast-forward pushes, requires PRs, two named checks, linear history, and has no bypass actors. It nevertheless has `required_approving_review_count: 0`, `require_last_push_approval: false`, and non-strict status checks. The alert correctly also reports no `CODEOWNERS`; no such file exists. The collaborators API returns exactly one direct collaborator, `Scetrov` (admin), so an independent required approval cannot currently be satisfied. This is not stale or false-positive. Formally record the high-risk exception; when a second maintainer exists, add `CODEOWNERS`, require at least one approving reviewer and latest-push approval, and enable strict/up-to-date status checks. `docs/threat-model.md:45-48` already states this intended control. |
| #43 Pinned-Dependencies | Medium | **Actionable improvement** | The current alert identifies `crates/evefrontier-service-route/Dockerfile:2`: `rust:1.97.0-bookworm` is tag-pinned but mutable, not digest-pinned. The API supplies the current manifest digest `sha256:8fa55b…c3073`. This Dockerfile is live local-build input via `docker-compose.yml:45-47`; the finding is current, not stale. |

API review evidence confirms merged PR #197 (supply-chain changes) had no reviews, and recent merged PR listing reports no review decisions. With only the one direct collaborator above, self-approval cannot provide independent review. The finding is accurate, not stale. The alert is accurate that no fuzz integration exists. It has a detection caveat: the alert documentation says Scorecard currently recognizes OSS-Fuzz/ClusterFuzzLite and only selected non-Rust language-native fuzzers, so a standalone Rust `cargo-fuzz` harness may improve assurance yet still not clear this Scorecard item. |

### Correct

- Scorecard is enabled on pushes to `main`, scheduled weekly, and manually dispatchable; its action and SARIF upload are immutable SHA references (`.github/workflows/scorecard.yml:3-33`).
- Third-party Actions are SHA pinned, including dependency review (`.github/workflows/dependency-review.yml:1-17`). The live repository API also reports Dependabot security updates, secret scanning, and secret-scanning push protection enabled.
- The active `mainline` ruleset already blocks deletion and non-fast-forward updates, limits merges to squash, requires PR-thread resolution, requires `Build and test workspace` and `Dependency review`, and has no bypass actors. Thus #42 is a narrow, material approval/CODEOWNERS gap rather than absence of all protection.
- Docker release has provenance enabled (`.github/workflows/docker-release.yml:187-202`), Trivy scanning with documented unfixed-vulnerability policy (`:220-245`), cosign signing (`:248-279`), and SBOM generation (`:281-313`).

### Blocker

- **High — independent review remains impossible today.** #42 and #49 should not be dismissed as false positives: GitHub API proves the single direct collaborator and zero required approvals, while the most recent security/governance PR #197 has no approval. The practical disposition is a documented, time-bounded single-maintainer exception plus recruiting a second reviewer; it is not an equivalent security control.

### Note

- The six Docker findings are genuine but are not exhaustive of mutable base-image use. `docker-release.yml:175-185` generates a release Dockerfile containing the same unpinned distroless `FROM` at line 178. Pin that reference in the same remediation, and use a controlled digest-update process (for example Docker ecosystem Dependabot configuration) so digest pins do not block security updates. This reconciles reproducibility with the existing docs’ update intent at `docs/SECURITY_AUDIT.md:260-280`.
- For #51, prioritize fuzz/property tests at parser/decompression boundaries: `decode_fmap_token` base64-decodes then unboundedly gzip-decompresses caller input (`crates/evefrontier-lib/src/fmap.rs:307-348`); archive extraction processes ZIP entries (`crates/evefrontier-lib/src/github.rs:931-968`); and spatial-index loading reads and zstd-decompresses serialized data (`crates/evefrontier-lib/src/spatial.rs:1114-1235`). Harnesses should assert no panic, bounded resource behavior, and clear `Err` results for malformed input. Cargo-fuzz plus an OSS-Fuzz/ClusterFuzzLite path is needed if clearing Scorecard itself is a goal.
- No project/source files were modified. The only written file is this required review artifact under the untracked `.pi-subagents/` area.
